questions.question: Who defined machine learning as 'the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed' in 1959?
questions.explanation: Arthur Samuel defined machine learning in 1959 as 'the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed.'

questions.question: According to Tom Mitchell's 1997 definition, what are the three components of a machine learning system?
options: Task T, Experience E, Performance measure P
questions.explanation: Tom Mitchell defined ML as: a computer program learns from experience E with respect to task T and performance measure P, if its performance on T improves with experience E.

questions.question: What was one of the first mainstream machine learning applications that improved lives of hundreds of millions of people in the 1990s?
questions.explanation: Spam filters were one of the first mainstream ML applications, helping hundreds of millions of people by automatically flagging unwanted emails.

questions.question: In machine learning, what is the term for examples that the system uses to learn?
questions.explanation: The training set consists of examples that the system uses to learn. Each example is called a training instance or sample.

questions.question: What is the part of a machine learning system that learns and makes predictions called?
questions.explanation: The model is the part of the ML system that learns and makes predictions. Neural networks and random forests are examples of models.

questions.question: In supervised learning, what are the desired solutions in the training set called?
questions.explanation: In supervised learning, the training data includes the desired solutions called labels. This is what the model learns to predict.

questions.question: What type of machine learning task involves predicting a target numeric value?
questions.explanation: Regression is the task of predicting a target numeric value, such as predicting the price of a car given its features.

questions.question: What is the common performance measure used in classification tasks?
questions.explanation: Accuracy is commonly used in classification tasks. It measures the ratio of correctly classified instances.

questions.question: In unsupervised learning, what is the state of the training data?
questions.explanation: In unsupervised learning, the training data is unlabeled. The system tries to learn patterns without any teacher or labels.

questions.question: What unsupervised learning task involves detecting groups of similar instances?
questions.explanation: Clustering is an unsupervised learning task that groups similar instances together without predefined labels.

questions.question: What is the goal of dimensionality reduction?
options: Simplify data without losing too much information
questions.explanation: Dimensionality reduction aims to simplify the data by reducing the number of features while preserving as much information as possible.

questions.question: What is feature extraction in the context of dimensionality reduction?
questions.explanation: Feature extraction involves merging several correlated features into one to reduce dimensionality while preserving information.

questions.question: What unsupervised task involves detecting unusual instances to prevent fraud?
questions.explanation: Anomaly detection is used to detect unusual instances such as fraudulent credit card transactions or manufacturing defects.

questions.question: What is the difference between anomaly detection and novelty detection?
options: Anomaly detection can handle outliers in training data, novelty detection requires clean training data
Novelty detection is supervised, anomaly detection is not
questions.explanation: Anomaly detection can handle some outliers in training data, while novelty detection requires a clean training set with no instances you want to detect.

questions.question: What unsupervised task discovers interesting relations between attributes in data?
questions.explanation: Association rule learning discovers interesting relations between attributes, such as discovering that people who buy barbecue sauce and chips also tend to buy steak.

questions.question: What is semi-supervised learning?
options: Learning with partially labeled data (mix of labeled and unlabeled)
questions.explanation: Semi-supervised learning deals with data that's partially labeled, having plenty of unlabeled instances and few labeled instances.

questions.question: What is self-supervised learning?
options: Generating fully labeled dataset from unlabeled data
questions.explanation: Self-supervised learning generates a fully labeled dataset from an unlabeled one, allowing supervised learning algorithms to be used afterward.

questions.question: In reinforcement learning, what is the learning system called?
questions.explanation: In reinforcement learning, the learning system is called an agent. It observes the environment, performs actions, and receives rewards.

questions.question: What is a policy in reinforcement learning?
options: The strategy that defines what action the agent should choose in a given situation
questions.explanation: A policy defines what action the agent should choose when it is in a given situation, aiming to maximize rewards over time.

questions.question: Which famous program used reinforcement learning to beat the world champion at Go?
questions.explanation: DeepMind's AlphaGo used reinforcement learning and beat Ke Jie, the world champion at Go, in May 2017.

questions.question: What is batch learning also called?
questions.explanation: Batch learning is also called offline learning because the system is trained offline using all available data, then launched into production without further learning.

questions.question: What phenomenon occurs when a model's performance decays over time because the world evolves while the model remains unchanged?
questions.explanation: Model rot or data drift occurs when a model's performance decays over time because the world continues to evolve while the model remains unchanged.

questions.question: What is online learning also known as?
questions.explanation: Online learning is also known as incremental learning because the system learns incrementally from incoming data.

questions.question: In online learning, what controls how fast the system adapts to changing data?
questions.explanation: The learning rate controls how fast an online learning system adapts to changing data. A high rate means rapid adaptation but quick forgetting of old data.

questions.question: What is out-of-core learning?
options: Training on huge datasets that cannot fit in main memory
questions.explanation: Out-of-core learning trains models on huge datasets that cannot fit in one machine's main memory by processing data in chunks.

questions.question: What are the two main approaches to generalization in machine learning?
questions.explanation: The two main approaches to generalization are instance-based learning (comparing new data to known examples) and model-based learning (building a predictive model).

questions.question: In instance-based learning, how does the system generalize to new cases?
options: By using a similarity measure to compare them to learned examples
questions.explanation: Instance-based learning generalizes to new cases by using a similarity measure to compare them to the learned examples (or a subset of them).

questions.question: What is model selection in model-based learning?
options: Choosing the color of the model
Choosing the type of model and fully specifying its architecture
questions.explanation: Model selection consists of choosing the type of model (e.g., linear regression) and fully specifying its architecture.

questions.question: What is training a model?
options: Running an algorithm to find model parameter values that minimize a cost function
Testing the model on new data
questions.explanation: Training a model means running an algorithm to find the model parameter values that will make it best fit the training data.

questions.question: What is the term for applying a trained model to make predictions on new cases?
questions.explanation: Inference is the process of applying a trained model to make predictions on new cases that the model has never seen before.

questions.question: According to the paper by Banko and Brill (2001), what matters more than algorithms for complex problems?
questions.explanation: Banko and Brill's 2001 paper showed that very different ML algorithms performed almost identically well once given enough data, suggesting data matters more than algorithms for complex problems.

questions.question: What is sampling noise?
options: Nonrepresentative data as a result of chance when the sample is too small
questions.explanation: Sampling noise is nonrepresentative data that results from chance when the sample size is too small.

questions.question: What is sampling bias?
options: Nonrepresentative samples due to flawed sampling method, even with large samples
questions.explanation: Sampling bias occurs when the sampling method is flawed, resulting in nonrepresentative samples even when the sample size is very large.

questions.question: What happened in the 1936 US presidential election poll by Literary Digest?
options: They predicted Landon would win with 57% but Roosevelt won with 62%
questions.explanation: Literary Digest predicted Landon would get 57% of votes, but Roosevelt won with 62%. The flaw was using telephone directories and magazine lists that favored wealthier Republican-leaning people.

questions.question: What is nonresponse bias?
options: When everyone responds to a poll
When less than 100% of polled people answer, potentially ruling out certain groups
When the sample is too large
questions.explanation: Nonresponse bias occurs when less than 100% of polled people answer, potentially ruling out people who don't care about the topic or don't like the polling organization.

questions.question: What is feature engineering?
options: Coming up with a good set of features to train on through selection, extraction, and creating new features
questions.explanation: Feature engineering involves feature selection (selecting most useful features), feature extraction (combining features), and creating new features by gathering new data.

questions.question: What is overfitting?
options: When a model performs well on training data but does not generalize well to new data
When a model performs poorly on training data
When a model is too simple
When there is too little data
questions.explanation: Overfitting occurs when a model performs well on training data but does not generalize well to new data, essentially learning the noise in the training data.

questions.question: What causes overfitting?
options: Model too complex relative to amount and noisiness of training data
questions.explanation: Overfitting happens when the model is too complex relative to the amount and noisiness of the training data, causing it to detect patterns in the noise.

questions.question: What is one solution to overfitting?
options: Simplify the model, gather more training data, or reduce noise in data
questions.explanation: Solutions to overfitting include simplifying the model (fewer parameters), gathering more training data, or reducing noise in the training data.

questions.question: What is regularization?
options: Adding more complexity to a model
Constraining a model to make it simpler and reduce the risk of overfitting
questions.explanation: Regularization is constraining a model to make it simpler and reduce the risk of overfitting, such as by limiting the values of parameters.

questions.question: What is a hyperparameter?
options: A parameter of the learning algorithm (not the model) set prior to training
questions.explanation: A hyperparameter is a parameter of the learning algorithm (not of the model). It is set prior to training and remains constant during training, like the regularization parameter.

questions.question: What is underfitting?
options: When a model is too complex
When a model is too simple to learn the underlying structure of the data
When a model has too much data
questions.explanation: Underfitting occurs when a model is too simple to learn the underlying structure of the data, resulting in inaccurate predictions even on training examples.

questions.question: What is a solution to underfitting?
options: Select a more powerful model with more parameters, or feed better features
questions.explanation: To fix underfitting, you can select a more powerful model with more parameters, feed better features to the algorithm, or reduce constraints on the model.

questions.question: What is the common split ratio for training and test sets?
questions.explanation: It is common to use 80% of the data for training and hold out 20% for testing, though this depends on the dataset size.

questions.question: What is generalization error also called?
questions.explanation: Generalization error (or out-of-sample error) is the error rate on new cases, telling you how well the model will perform on instances it has never seen.

questions.question: If training error is low but generalization error is high, what does it indicate?
questions.explanation: If training error is low but generalization error is high, it means the model is overfitting the training data - performing well on training data but poorly on new data.

questions.question: What is holdout validation?
options: Holding out part of the training set to evaluate candidate models
questions.explanation: Holdout validation involves holding out part of the training set to evaluate several candidate models and select the best one before final testing.

questions.question: What problem occurs if you measure generalization error multiple times on the test set and adapt the model accordingly?
options: The model is unlikely to perform as well on new data because it was adapted to that specific test set
questions.explanation: If you adapt the model to the test set by trying multiple configurations, the model becomes overfitted to that specific test set and won't generalize well to truly new data.

questions.question: What is data mining?
options: Digging into large amounts of data to discover hidden patterns
questions.explanation: Data mining is the process of digging into large amounts of data to discover hidden patterns. Machine learning excels at this task.

questions.question: What is transfer learning?
options: Moving data from one computer to another
Transferring knowledge from one task to another
questions.explanation: Transfer learning is transferring knowledge from one task to another. It's one of the most important techniques in machine learning today, especially with deep neural networks.
